metadata.title: Supplemental Material for D-I-WHAT? Identifying Creative Domains in Do-It-Yourself Videos on YouTube
metadata.journal: Psychology of Aesthetics, Creativity, and the Arts

abstract: Online worlds offer a massive display of people's lives including their creative activities. In the present study, we investigated do-it-yourself (DIY) videos on YouTube to explore the type and prevalence of everyday creative behaviors. DIY is a term associated with the production of original and effective products, and, like YouTube, typically features amateur-generated content. As such, this setting allowed us to gain insight into the creative behavior that happens in our homes, garages, and front yards. Using two topic modeling approaches, structural topic model (STM) and BERTopic, we analyzed about 13,000 videos from three mainly English-speaking countries (Australia, the United Kingdom, and the United States) that were published between 2019 and 2021. In a final set of 5,618 videos including transcripts, STM identified 21 topics, such as Decoration, Garden, Interior Design, and Textiles, which are partly similar to, yet also extend established creativity domains. BERTopic identified 35 slightly more narrow topics of creative activities (e.g., Facemasks, Accessories, Candles) that offer additional insights into the actual diversity of everyday creative behavior. Further analyses uncovered plausible time dynamics of specific topics and examined the overlap and differences of the two chosen modeling approaches. Our results indicate that leveraging data-driven approaches to study creativity in online environments complements traditional methods in offering new insights into both the diversity and prevalent domains of everyday creative behavior.

paragraphs: Welcome to the Internet, have a look around, anything that brain of yours can think of can be found, we've got mountains of content-some better, some worse, if none of it's of interest to you, you'd be the first. (Welcome to the Internet-Bo Burnham)

We are amidst a digital revolution that permeates all spheres of life, affecting how we work, communicate, and connect with others. The expression of creativity is by no means an exception, as it is reshaped by the technological and social means of the digital environment (Literat & Glăveanu, 2016), be it new creative products (e.g., memes; Glǎveanu & de Saint Laurent, 2021;Literat, 2019;Literat & van den Berg, 2019), large-scale creative collaboration (Ceh & Lebuda, 2022), or, generally, new modalities of creative action (e.g., virtual reality; Bourgeois-Bougrine et al., 2020). Consequently, creativity research is challenged to keep up with trends and general differences in the way creativity is expressed online. What creative activities are people performing amidst a rapidly evolving environment filled with digital technology? Platforms such as YouTube enable users to quickly share the process and results of their creative activities online (Ceh & Benedek, 2021). The masses of freely available content posted online offer a unique opportunity for research to study creative behavior at a large scale. The present work investigates a prominent sphere on YouTube that reflects everyday creative behavior in online worlds: do-it-yourself (DIY) videos (Francisco-Maffezzolli et al., 2021). DIY is a term associated with the production of original and useful goods (Fox, 2013), which conforms to the definition of creative products (Runco & Jaeger, 2012). DIY videos can thus be viewed as a window to the diversity of creative behaviors. Following a bottom-up approach, we aim to capture creative activities that people pursue and the products they create, which could be missed by topdown approaches (i.e., when using inventories of creative behavior asking for a limited set of creative activities). Using natural language processing methods on a data set of DIY videos, we assessed the prevalence and dimensionality of creative activities showcased on YouTube and then examined the resulting creative domains for trends over time.

According to YouTube, users watch over 1 billion hours of content every day (YouTube, 2022). Although the platform is constantly evolving and expanding its portfolio (e.g., to music streaming; cf. YouTube Music), its roots remain: YouTube is a platform to upload, watch, and explore video content. From this premise, a diverse culture of creating has developed, as evidenced in various trends that are strongly associated with the platform (e.g., vlogging; Stein et al., 2022) and huge amounts of data constantly being uploaded to the platform (more than 500 hr of material per minute; YouTube, 2022). Worth noting, YouTube does not restrain specific kinds of content (i.e., styles, nor materials), thus, it is considered the archetype of the digital creative platform (Gauntlett, 2018). Given the availability of options to interact and evaluate on platforms such as YouTube (e.g., through commenting and liking), it can be assumed that these platforms affect how one views one's own and others' creativity, especially when considering creativity as a social construct (Literat & Glăveanu, 2016).

These remarks, however, follow the assumption that content posted on YouTube is, in fact, creative to some extent. This is not a trivial premise, as there are many lenses through which creativity may or may not be seen in a YouTube video. On a surface level, the mere creation (i.e., production) of a video, independent of what it contains, may be considered a creative act in the digital domain that can be of varying quality (e.g., due to the production or editing process; Stein et al., 2022). Then, of course, the actual content of a video plays an independent role-after all, it could range from something completely mundane to highly original work. The creative content may still take various forms, from sharing ideas over actual creative behaviors, to final products, and any combination thereof. Finally, how a video is presented on the platform could add to its creative quality: Users can embed their videos creatively, picking a catchy title, or drafting a creative description. In summary, the creativity of a user's original work on YouTube can vary at the level of production, process, and release effort (cf. Henriksen & Hoelting, 2017;Literat, 2019). Importantly, YouTube is the most popular platform for consuming creative content and associated with a broad range of creative domains, whereas other platforms often feature more specific content (e.g., Flickr focuses on photography), have a smaller community, or involve a less versatile modality to convey content (Ceh & Benedek, 2021). YouTube offers users much freedom to share any kind of creative activity. In fact, creative self-expression and the possibility to expose one's creative vein are among the strongest motives for sharing creative work on online platforms, and people frequently engage in such activities (Ceh & Benedek, 2021), suggesting that YouTube offers a diverse and domain-general display of creative behavior.

Do-it-yourself is a term with rich history. This is best reflected in DIY's ascription to initial human survival (Kuznetsov & Paulos, 2010): crafting, repairing, and repurposing are undoubtedly pillars of our existence. Kuznetsov and Paulos (2010) provide a great overview over a term that has been around for a long time and has come to increased attention-namely by the rise of online communities and environments (e.g., social media platforms). Initially being a form of counterculture (McKay, 1998), doing-it-yourself has become a societal phenomenon (Stangler & Maxwell, 2012) connected to producing original and economical physical goods, as well as assembling them and putting them to use (i.e., new-DIY paradigm, Fox, 2013). Creativity researchers typically deem a product creative when it is both novel/original and useful/effective (Runco & Jaeger, 2012), the latter often being aligned with the field of application (e.g., a painting may be effective in the sense that it resonates with the observer, creating inspiration and sparking certain emotions). Clearly, there is substantial definitional overlap between DIY practices and what is generally considered creative. Furthermore, YouTube is a platform that mostly features amateurcreated content (Arthurs et al., 2018), and DIY communities typically consist of hobbyists and enthusiasts that have learned to perform specialist tasks (C. T. Wolf, 2016)-so-called expert amateurs (Kuznetsov & Paulos, 2010)-but only few professionals. This congruency makes it likely that a huge proportion of content within DIY videos on YouTube will mirror the dedicated creative work by nonprofessionals we typically refer to when speaking of everyday creative behavior (Ilha Villanova & Pina e Cunha, 2021;Kaufman & Beghetto, 2009;Kaufman & Glăveanu, 2022;Richards et al., 1988). Thus, investigating the content of DIY videos on YouTube may indeed yield a good general estimation of everyday creative behavior within the population of digitally savvy individuals.

Until now, investigations of DIY content have not explicitly focused on creative content dimensions as a whole but still offer useful insights into the peculiarities of this sort of content on YouTube. In one study, DIY home repair content viewers were interviewed regarding their information-seeking behavior (C. T. Wolf, 2016) and three main aspects were elaborated on: first, the ease of information and knowledge acquisition through the visual medium video was highlighted, emphasizing that videos often contain helpful contextual information (e.g., not only what tool is needed, but also how it looks and how it is correctly put to use) that cannot equally well be transferred through print or audio. Thus, watching DIY videos on YouTube may be a particularly fruitful source for creative learning because it leverages the video medium. Second, it was shown that viewers use these videos to assess their own self-confidence and selfefficacy regarding a DIY task: Will they be able to repair an object themselves or does the conveyed complexity exceed their perceived abilities? Therefore, knowledge transfer through watching DIY videos on YouTube stimulates self-other comparison and evaluation processes, implicating the importance of creative metacognition and creative self-belief (Karwowski et al., 2019). Third, interviewees also reported to assess the credibility of videos by focusing on both the process and result, stating that user-generated videos have a varying degree of quality-some being of high quality while others were deemed less useful. This indicates that viewers discern the degree of effectivity and appropriateness, and these judgments may benefit from seeing the process rather than just seeing the final product. This evaluation is important in light of evidence that there is low quality, in fact, even dubious content available: Guo et al. (2020) investigated orthodontic-related videos on YouTube and found DIY videos about creating fake braces or self-removing real ones, and self-treating other dental issues. Consequently, this digital space requires users to discriminate obscure, low-quality content from educational and inspirational videos about creative products. An investigation of 103 digital influencer channels over 12 years found that two of the top 10 content dimensions referred to DIY and activities related to decorating and organizing (Francisco-Maffezzolli et al., 2021). While these findings provide a first impression of the impact of DIY content on YouTube users, the diversity of content, and the high prevalence on the platform, it also sparks curiosity on the type of content captured within the banner of DIY. It has become more and more of a habit to inform the world about our creative endeavors using online platforms. With the availability of great amounts of data, researchers can explore this digital avenue of everyday creativity and investigate trends and phenomena associated with it and compare it with established domains of creative behavior that are otherwise typically assessed by means of self-report.
Self-report measures of creative behavior rely on predefined items and overarching domains (Silvia et al., 2012, Weiss et al., 2021).

One of the earliest measures, the Creative Behavior Inventory (CBI; Hocevar, 1979), originally had 90 items collapsed into six subscales (i.e., Fine Arts, Crafts, Literature, Music, Performing Arts, and Mathematics-Science) but is now predominantly used as a short version with 28 items (Dollinger, 2003) that is considered unidimensional (Rodriguez et al., 2021). The Kaufman Domains of Creativity Scale (K-DOCS; Kaufman, 2012) uses 50 items to indicate creative engagement across five domains (Self/Everyday, Scholarly, Performance, Mechanical/Scientific, and Artistic). The Biographical Inventory of Creative Behaviors (BICB; Batey, 2007) uses 34 items and can be viewed as unidimensional (Silvia et al., 2021). The Inventory of Creative Activities and Achievements (ICAA; Diedrich et al., 2018) assesses creative activities and achievement at eight domains (Literature, Music, Arts-and-Crafts, Creative Cooking, Sports, Visual Arts, Performing Arts, and Science & Engineering). The Creative Achievement Questionnaire (CAQ; Carson et al., 2005) is another selfreport measure that assesses creative achievements at 10 domains (e.g., Music, Dance, Humor, Architectural Design, Scientific Discovery) that form three underlying factors (i.e., expressive, performance, and scientific dimensions of creative achievement).

Taken together, these inventories capture a great variety of creative behavior, but also reveal inconsistencies regarding what domains are included, how broadly they are assessed (e.g., Arts vs. Visual Arts), and at what level of domain-expertise (e.g., making up rhymes vs. my theatrical work has been recognized in a national publication). In fact, creativity research commonly distinguished between Little-c, Pro-c, and Big-C creativity to refer to everyday creativity, professional-level creativity, and eminent creativity (Kaufman & Beghetto, 2009), and it was proposed that inventories of creative behaviors (I often engage in XY) are better suited for Little-c samples, whereas inventories of creative achievements (my creative work received a prize) are better suited for Pro-c samples (Diedrich et al., 2018). There has been much debate to what extent creativity is domain-general (certain skills are relevant across all creative domains) or domain-specific (creativity in one domain does not predict creativity in another domain; cf. Baer, 1998;Plucker, 1998). More recent theorizing aims to bridge this gap by assuming that creativity includes both domain-general and domain-specific aspects. For example, the Amusement Park theoretical model of creativity (Baer & Kaufman, 2005) proposes that creativity involves some domain-general, initial requirements such as motivation or basic cognitive ability, but also includes more specific thematic areas, domains, and microdomains. In this context, more specialized interests and skills are thought to become more important at higher levels of creative expertise (Kaufman, & Beghetto, 2009) as increasing differentiation and specialization results from ongoing commitment in a specific domain (Barbot & Tinio, 2015).

On the one hand, self-report measures of creativity are more manageable than asking for creative behaviors in a bottom-up fashion, such as sometimes done in experience sampling methods and qualitative research (Silvia & Cotter, 2021), and thus reflect an efficient approach to obtain a quick estimate of individual differences in common creative activities. On the other hand, the need to confine the scope to predefined behaviors and domains comes with the risk of disregarding many, especially nonmainstream or emerging forms of creative behavior, which may reduce the precision of assessment and potentially even implies systematic biases such as cohort effects. One recent study asked people to openly report creative activities they engage in their leisure time, and found that they can be organized by nine domains (i.e., Literature, Music, Handicraft, Interior and garden design, Creative cooking, Visual art, Performing art, Science/ Technology, and Social) which overlap with previous classifications yet also revealed substantial differences in domain prevalence (25.9% handicraft vs. 2.8% performing arts; Benedek et al., 2020). Another study found that creative online postings showed a largely similar prevalence for most domains compared to offline behavior, but also differed in others such as posting creative content related to interior and garden design was less frequent online whereas an opposing trend was found for literature (Ceh & Benedek, 2021). What is more, the emergence of new technologies, mostly related to digitalization, might not be adequately captured in some, if not most measures. Digitalization challenges the established measures in many ways, for example, when new forms of creative behavior (e.g., creativity in computer games, producing podcasts, computer-based generative art) emerge that are not easily attributed to existing creative domains, either because they imply a blend across multiple domains or because they make for a distinct domain that is not adequately addressed in the present measure. Consequently, creativity research could benefit from a broader understanding of the content of creative activities shared online and use it to develop new and improve existing measures (Silvia et al., 2021). Such an investigation may benefit from a data-driven approach that is not constrained by assumptions inherent in established creativity measures (e.g., assumed set of creative domains).

On a surface level, the mere creation (i.e., production) of a video, independent of what it contains, may be considered a creative act in the digital domain that can be of varying quality (e.g., due to the production or editing process; Stein et al., 2022). Then, of course, the actual content of a video plays an independent role-after all, it could range from something completely mundane to highly original work. The creative content may still take various forms, from sharing ideas over actual creative behaviors, to final products, and any combination thereof. Finally, how a video is presented on the platform could add to its creative quality: Users can embed their videos creatively, picking a catchy title, or drafting a creative description. In summary, the creativity of a user's original work on YouTube can vary at the level of production, process, and release effort (cf. Henriksen & Hoelting, 2017;Literat, 2019). Importantly, YouTube is the most popular platform for consuming creative content and associated with a broad range of creative domains, whereas other platforms often feature more specific content (e.g., Flickr focuses on photography), have a smaller community, or involve a less versatile modality to convey content (Ceh & Benedek, 2021). YouTube offers users much freedom to share any kind of creative activity. In fact, creative self-expression and the possibility to expose one's creative vein are among the strongest motives for sharing creative work on online platforms, and people frequently engage in such activities (Ceh & Benedek, 2021), suggesting that YouTube offers a diverse and domain-general display of creative behavior.

Do-it-yourself is a term with rich history. This is best reflected in DIY's ascription to initial human survival (Kuznetsov & Paulos, 2010): crafting, repairing, and repurposing are undoubtedly pillars of our existence. Kuznetsov and Paulos (2010) provide a great overview over a term that has been around for a long time and has come to increased attention-namely by the rise of online communities and environments (e.g., social media platforms). Initially being a form of counterculture (McKay, 1998), doing-it-yourself has become a societal phenomenon (Stangler & Maxwell, 2012) connected to producing original and economical physical goods, as well as assembling them and putting them to use (i.e., new-DIY paradigm, Fox, 2013). Creativity researchers typically deem a product creative when it is both novel/original and useful/effective (Runco & Jaeger, 2012), the latter often being aligned with the field of application (e.g., a painting may be effective in the sense that it resonates with the observer, creating inspiration and sparking certain emotions). Clearly, there is substantial definitional overlap between DIY practices and what is generally considered creative. Furthermore, YouTube is a platform that mostly features amateurcreated content (Arthurs et al., 2018), and DIY communities typically consist of hobbyists and enthusiasts that have learned to perform specialist tasks (C. T. Wolf, 2016)-so-called expert amateurs (Kuznetsov & Paulos, 2010)-but only few professionals. This congruency makes it likely that a huge proportion of content within DIY videos on YouTube will mirror the dedicated creative work by nonprofessionals we typically refer to when speaking of everyday creative behavior (Ilha Villanova & Pina e Cunha, 2021;Kaufman & Beghetto, 2009;Kaufman & Glăveanu, 2022;Richards et al., 1988). Thus, investigating the content of DIY videos on YouTube may indeed yield a good general estimation of everyday creative behavior within the population of digitally savvy individuals.

Until now, investigations of DIY content have not explicitly focused on creative content dimensions as a whole but still offer useful insights into the peculiarities of this sort of content on YouTube. In one study, DIY home repair content viewers were interviewed regarding their information-seeking behavior (C. T. Wolf, 2016) and three main aspects were elaborated on: first, the ease of information and knowledge acquisition through the visual medium video was highlighted, emphasizing that videos often contain helpful contextual information (e.g., not only what tool is needed, but also how it looks and how it is correctly put to use) that cannot equally well be transferred through print or audio. Thus, watching DIY videos on YouTube may be a particularly fruitful source for creative learning because it leverages the video medium. Second, it was shown that viewers use these videos to assess their own self-confidence and selfefficacy regarding a DIY task: Will they be able to repair an object themselves or does the conveyed complexity exceed their perceived abilities? Therefore, knowledge transfer through watching DIY videos on YouTube stimulates self-other comparison and evaluation processes, implicating the importance of creative metacognition and creative self-belief (Karwowski et al., 2019). Third, interviewees also reported to assess the credibility of videos by focusing on both the process and result, stating that user-generated videos have a varying degree of quality-some being of high quality while others were deemed less useful. This indicates that viewers discern the degree of effectivity and appropriateness, and these judgments may benefit from seeing the process rather than just seeing the final product. This evaluation is important in light of evidence that there is low quality, in fact, even dubious content available: Guo et al. (2020) investigated orthodontic-related videos on YouTube and found DIY videos about creating fake braces or self-removing real ones, and self-treating other dental issues. Consequently, this digital space requires users to discriminate obscure, low-quality content from educational and inspirational videos about creative products. An investigation of 103 digital influencer channels over 12 years found that two of the top 10 content dimensions referred to DIY and activities related to decorating and organizing (Francisco-Maffezzolli et al., 2021). While these findings provide a first impression of the impact of DIY content on YouTube users, the diversity of content, and the high prevalence on the platform, it also sparks curiosity on the type of content captured within the banner of DIY. It has become more and more of a habit to inform the world about our creative endeavors using online platforms. With the availability of great amounts of data, researchers can explore this digital avenue of everyday creativity and investigate trends and phenomena associated with it and compare it with established domains of creative behavior that are otherwise typically assessed by means of self-report.

Self-report measures of creative behavior rely on predefined items and overarching domains (Silvia et al., 2012, Weiss et al., 2021).

One of the earliest measures, the Creative Behavior Inventory (CBI; Hocevar, 1979), originally had 90 items collapsed into six subscales (i.e., Fine Arts, Crafts, Literature, Music, Performing Arts, and Mathematics-Science) but is now predominantly used as a short version with 28 items (Dollinger, 2003) that is considered unidimensional (Rodriguez et al., 2021). The Kaufman Domains of Creativity Scale (K-DOCS; Kaufman, 2012) uses 50 items to indicate creative engagement across five domains (Self/Everyday, Scholarly, Performance, Mechanical/Scientific, and Artistic). The Biographical Inventory of Creative Behaviors (BICB; Batey, 2007) uses 34 items and can be viewed as unidimensional (Silvia et al., 2021). The Inventory of Creative Activities and Achievements (ICAA; Diedrich et al., 2018) assesses creative activities and achievement at eight domains (Literature, Music, Arts-and-Crafts, Creative Cooking, Sports, Visual Arts, Performing Arts, and Science & Engineering). The Creative Achievement Questionnaire (CAQ; Carson et al., 2005) is another selfreport measure that assesses creative achievements at 10 domains (e.g., Music, Dance, Humor, Architectural Design, Scientific Discovery) that form three underlying factors (i.e., expressive, performance, and scientific dimensions of creative achievement).

Taken together, these inventories capture a great variety of creative behavior, but also reveal inconsistencies regarding what domains are included, how broadly they are assessed (e.g., Arts vs. Visual Arts), and at what level of domain-expertise (e.g., making up rhymes vs. my theatrical work has been recognized in a national publication). In fact, creativity research commonly distinguished between Little-c, Pro-c, and Big-C creativity to refer to everyday creativity, professional-level creativity, and eminent creativity (Kaufman & Beghetto, 2009), and it was proposed that inventories of creative behaviors (I often engage in XY) are better suited for Little-c samples, whereas inventories of creative achievements (my creative work received a prize) are better suited for Pro-c samples (Diedrich et al., 2018). There has been much debate to what extent creativity is domain-general (certain skills are relevant across all creative domains) or domain-specific (creativity in one domain does not predict creativity in another domain; cf. Baer, 1998;Plucker, 1998). More recent theorizing aims to bridge this gap by assuming that creativity includes both domain-general and domain-specific aspects. For example, the Amusement Park theoretical model of creativity (Baer & Kaufman, 2005) proposes that creativity involves some domain-general, initial requirements such as motivation or basic cognitive ability, but also includes more specific thematic areas, domains, and microdomains. In this context, more specialized interests and skills are thought to become more important at higher levels of creative expertise (Kaufman, & Beghetto, 2009) as increasing differentiation and specialization results from ongoing commitment in a specific domain (Barbot & Tinio, 2015).

On the one hand, self-report measures of creativity are more manageable than asking for creative behaviors in a bottom-up fashion, such as sometimes done in experience sampling methods and qualitative research (Silvia & Cotter, 2021), and thus reflect an efficient approach to obtain a quick estimate of individual differences in common creative activities. On the other hand, the need to confine the scope to predefined behaviors and domains comes with the risk of disregarding many, especially nonmainstream or emerging forms of creative behavior, which may reduce the precision of assessment and potentially even implies systematic biases such as cohort effects. One recent study asked people to openly report creative activities they engage in their leisure time, and found that they can be organized by nine domains (i.e., Literature, Music, Handicraft, Interior and garden design, Creative cooking, Visual art, Performing art, Science/ Technology, and Social) which overlap with previous classifications yet also revealed substantial differences in domain prevalence (25.9% handicraft vs. 2.8% performing arts; Benedek et al., 2020). Another study found that creative online postings showed a largely similar prevalence for most domains compared to offline behavior, but also differed in others such as posting creative content related to interior and garden design was less frequent online whereas an opposing trend was found for literature (Ceh & Benedek, 2021). What is more, the emergence of new technologies, mostly related to digitalization, might not be adequately captured in some, if not most measures. Digitalization challenges the established measures in many ways, for example, when new forms of creative behavior (e.g., creativity in computer games, producing podcasts, computer-based generative art) emerge that are not easily attributed to existing creative domains, either because they imply a blend across multiple domains or because they make for a distinct domain that is not adequately addressed in the present measure. Consequently, creativity research could benefit from a broader understanding of the content of creative activities shared online and use it to develop new and improve existing measures (Silvia et al., 2021). Such an investigation may benefit from a data-driven approach that is not constrained by assumptions inherent in established creativity measures (e.g., assumed set of creative domains).

sections.paper_id: Ceh et al. - 2025 - D-I-WHAT Identifying creative domains in do-it-yourself videos on YouTube.
paragraphs: Building on the premise that the transformation to digital worlds such as online platforms and virtual reality is ongoing, irreversible, and impacting both individuals (C. T. Wolf, 2016) and society as a whole (Stangler & Maxwell, 2012), it is time for creativity research to take a digital step forward and investigate these new environments (even if researchers must sacrifice work hours on YouTube in diligent pursuit of this task). As YouTube is characterized by a high proportion of amateur-created content (Arthurs et al., 2018), and because of the creative and diverse nature of DIY content (Fox, 2013), an investigation of DIY videos will allow for a timely coverage of everyday creative behavior supported by digital methods, offering easy access to the wealth and diversity of creative behavior readily shared online. We aim to assess creative behaviors YouTube users are "doing themselves" in a bottom-up data-driven fashion and examine how they organize into discriminable topics. This enables to take stock of the diversity of creative behavior and helps to potentially identify new and trending creative topics (Silvia et al., 2021). To this end, we collected a set of DIY videos from three English-speaking countries between 2019 and 2022 and apply natural language processing techniques to identify discriminable topics (i.e., creative domains) that reflect prevalent everyday creative behavior, and explore them with respect to time trends.

sections.paper_id: Ceh et al. - 2025 - D-I-WHAT Identifying creative domains in do-it-yourself videos on YouTube.
paragraphs: We used the YouTube Data API v3 to collect data for this study. The application programming interface (API) allows to conduct detailed searches via accessing the YouTube search engine. We retrospectively searched a 3-year time period for videos that had the term "DIY" in their title. The search focused on three different English-speaking geographical locations across three continents to increase representativeness and enable tests of consistency. As geographical centers for the search, we picked major cities in the United Kingdom (Manchester; radius = 400 km), the United States (Philadelphia, PA; radius = 400 km), and Australia (Sydney; radius = 1,000 km). This search yielded a total of 13,021 videos.

For each video, we obtained the title, description, and tags. As the intended analyses benefit from greater amounts of text (Arthurs et al., 2018;Tang et al., 2014), we also got ahold of video transcripts using youtubecaption (Seo & Choi, 2020) when available (transcripts were not available for all videos, for example, when a video did not contain speech). While each video had a title, 3.73% of all videos did not have a description, 22.50% did not contain tags, and 39.90% did not allow for the extraction of transcripts. We explored which combinations of the available textual sources would provide the highest-quality results without diminishing the corpus size too much (see the online supplementary materials). For example, we found the video descriptions to contain a lot of noise and irrelevant information (e.g., affiliate links, selfpromotion), thus deciding to not consider them further. We only used videos containing a title, tags, and transcript (N = 6,319). Although we used English-speaking countries as geographical search constraints, not every video would contain English language. Thus, we used fastText (Mouselimis, 2022) to identify and remove 23 (0.36%) non-English videos.

Each video is linked to a user's channel. The videos were associated with a total of 3,599 unique channels. On average, a channel contributed 1.75 videos (SD = 2.63, min = 1, max = 68). We randomly selected a maximum of six videos per channel to ensure that overly active channels would not distort the data set (e.g., by only uploading very specific content). This led to the removal of additional 701 videos (11.09%). For all remaining videos (N = 5,618; Australia: n = 585, 10.41%; the United Kingdom: n = 2,499, 44.48%, the United States: n = 2,534, 45.11%) we compiled the total available text information from the title, tags, and transcript.

paragraphs: Text preprocessing is used to reduce the complexity of text input data while trying to avoid negatively affecting the interpretability of the resulting output. In topic modeling, researchers must consider various settings, such as the number of topics, hyperparameter configuration, or textual preprocessing steps, suggesting that topic modeling is not an out-of-the-box tool (Denny & Spirling, 2018;Lee et al., 2017;Maier et al., 2018). The first step of text preprocessing included removing audio description embedded in the transcripts (e.g., "[Music]" would appear in transcripts when music played). We then removed special characters (e.g., punctuation) and performed lower-case transformation. As the present study drew from two topic modeling approaches (i.e., structural topic model [STM], Roberts et al., 2014;BERTopic, Grootendorst, 2022) the subsequent preprocessing steps differed between the employed models. Specifically, for BERTopic, no additional preprocessing steps were performed to allow the document embeddings to be created as accurately as possible. Instead, we removed English stop words and highly frequent words after the embeddings were created and documents were reduced and clustered (Grootendorst, 2022). For BERTopic, the average initial document size was 1,352.20 words (Mdn = 994.50, SD = 1,525.14). For STM, prior to analysis, we additionally removed English stop words, digits, and single characters and trimmed white spaces. To further increase information density and topic interpretability, we eliminated topic-indiscriminate words (e.g., yeah, gonna, subscribe) that were among the 100 most frequently used words within the corpus (cf. Yang et al., 2016) and performed lemmatization. To reduce the computational effort, we trimmed the document-frequency matrix with a sparsity criterion of 0.99. After completing the preprocessing steps outlined above, a document prepared for STM analysis contained 263.18 words on average (Mdn = 200; SD = 270.07).

paragraphs: This study leveraged the STM (Roberts et al., 2014) and its implementation in R (stm; Roberts et al., 2019) to perform topic modeling based on latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA; Blei et al., 2003), a frequently used unsupervised-learning method to identify and model latent thematic clusters in a text corpus (Blei, 2012;Maier et al., 2018;Vayansky & Kumar, 2020). Each topic is a cluster of relevant words in the analyzed corpus, and the documents that make up the corpus correspond to finite mixtures of K topics (Panichella, 2021), making it a mixed-membership approach. In STM, the probability for each word w to occur in each topic K (β) and the probability for each document d to occur in each topic K (γ) are calculated from a term-by-document matrix (Maier et al., 2018;Panichella, 2021). Researchers can identify which words and documents represent certain topics. STM extends and outperforms classic LDA models (Roberts, Stewart, & Tingley, 2016), allowing for topics to be correlated, similar to the correlated topic model (CTM; Blei & Lafferty, 2007;Roberts, Stewart, & Airoldi, 2016), and for document-level covariates to be entered into the model (cf. Bai et al., 2021;Lucas et al., 2015;Roberts, Stewart, & Airoldi, 2016). We specified month of publication and geographical location as covariates. We initialized the STM using the recommended spectral LDA approach on the preprocessed corpus of text (Roberts et al., 2019), after transforming it to the required matrix format using the dfm function of the package tidytext (Silge & Robinson, 2016). We computed models for 5-75 topics, as the amount of topics K needs to be set in advance, and this choice takes the proposed range for small (a few hundred to a few thousand documents) and medium-sized corpora (10,000-100,000 documents; Roberts et al., 2019) into account. K defines the model's granularity: a too high K may result in very specific topics that are partly overlapping, whereas a too small K may result in overly general topics that lack specificity (cf. Maier et al., 2018). Coherence measures often serve as quantitative proxies for a topic's interpretability. Topic coherence measures the degree of semantic similarity between high scoring words in the topic (Stevens et al., 2012). STM relies on a coherence measure that is maximized when the most probable words in a given topic frequently co-occur together (Mimno et al., 2011;Newman et al., 2010;Roberts et al., 2019). Importantly, this metric is a reasonable surrogate for human judgments of topic quality (Mimno et al., 2011;Omar et al., 2015), but high semantic coherence alone does not implicate that multiple topics can be well discerned. For example, a topic model consisting only of few topics that are dominated by a few common words would yield high semantic coherence, but little interpretative value. Accordingly, words with high probabilities under topic i should have low probabilities under topic j (i.e., topic i is exclusive). In STM, exclusivity is approached via the FREX-metric (i.e., FRequency-EXclusivity; Bischof & Airoldi, 2012), which balances word frequency (to ensure that the word is representative of the topic) with its exclusivity (to ensure that the word is specific to the topic). As coherent and exclusive topics are considered to be more interpretable (Roberts et al., 2014), we considered the tradeoff between semantic coherence and exclusivity of topics as the main criterion upon deciding K (see Figures S1 and S2 in the online supplementary materials).

sections.paper_id: Ceh et al. - 2025 - D-I-WHAT Identifying creative domains in do-it-yourself videos on YouTube.
paragraphs: One issue with LDA-based topic modeling approaches is that they represent documents as a bag-of-words and disregard the semantic relationship between words. As a second topic modeling method, we used BERTopic (Grootendorst, 2022) and its implementation in Python (Grootendorst, 2020). Bidirectional encoder representations from transformers (BERT; Devlin et al., 2019) models have produced state-of-the-art results in natural language processing tasks and, unlike bag-of-words approaches, consider the context of words (i.e., they produce contextualized embeddings; Rogers et al., 2020). When initializing BERTopic, each document is embedded in a dense vector space with the assumption that semantically similar (i.e., close) documents represent the same topic (Grootendorst, 2022). Within the Sentence-BERT framework (Reimers & Gurevych, 2019), we used the all-mpnet-base-v2 model to create the sentence embeddings. Importantly, this model has a token limit of 384, meaning that longer texts are truncated. When documents contain multiple topics, this may lead to inaccurate representations. However, when screening the videos, we found the vast majority to be about single types of content (i.e., one topic per video). In consequence, the title, tags, and truncated parts of the transcript should lead to accurate representations. We tested this assumption for a STM where we limited the document length to match the token limit imposed by the language model and found the resulting topics to be highly similar by means of composition (e.g., 21 vs. 23 topics with high overlap) and interpretability. After creating the embeddings, BERTopic uses UMAP to reduce the dimensionality of the document embeddings (McInnes et al., 2018) and clusters the reduced embeddings using HDBSCAN (McInnes et al., 2017). To generate the topic representations, the documents within each cluster are combined and a TF-IDF procedure is applied to the previously estimated document clusters to understand which words are especially relevant (cf. Grootendorst, 2022). To increase the interpretability of the generated topics, English stop words were removed after the embeddings were created, and the impact of frequent terms for the topical estimation was reduced (i.e., by taking the square root of the bag-of-words after matrix normalization; Grootendorst, 2022;Pedregosa, 2011). Note that these steps do not affect the previously generated embeddings (Grootendorst, 2022). We ran a set of BERTopic models where we varied the hyperparameters top_n_words (7, 10, 13) and min_topic_size (10,20,30,40,50). We additionally computed a coherence measure (normalized (pointwise) mutual information [NPMI]; Bouma, 2009), which is based on normalized mutual pointwise information and correlates highly with human topic coherence ratings (Röder et al., 2015) and a topic diversity measure which is based on the percentage of unique words for each of the resulting topic models (Dieng et al., 2020). For the choice of a model, we considered the subjective interpretability of topics alongside the estimated quantitative measures.

sections.paper_id: Ceh et al. - 2025 - D-I-WHAT Identifying creative domains in do-it-yourself videos on YouTube.
Ceh et al. - 2025 - D-I-WHAT Identifying creative domains in do-it-yourself videos on YouTube.
paragraphs: Applying STM to the video data, we identified K = 11 and K = 21 to be suitable model candidates, showing both high coherence and exclusivity across all topics (see Figures S1 and S2 in the online supplementary materials). We opted for the model with K = 21, which left us with more specific and easily interpretable topics. Separate analyses with the topic model with K = 21 on location-based subsets of the data (i.e., United States vs. United Kingdom) yielded highly similar topic results, thus indicating reliable topic estimations with the present choice of K. To understand and label the 21 topics resulting from STM, we considered the top words for each topic based on the word-topic probability matrix and the FREX score.

The 21 topics reflect a broad diversity of everyday creative activities in DIY videos and are listed in Table 1. First, some of the identified topics blend well with established creative domains. For example, topics such as Papercrafts, Textiles, Accessories, and Decoration are related to what is commonly considered Handicrafts. The topic Recipes resembles the domain of Creative Cooking. The topic Interior Design has a clear focus on furniture and is dedicated to makeover projects. Complementary, the topic Garden focuses on DIY activities in backyards. Together, they make up what has been referred to as Interior/Garden Design. Similarly, Second, some topics are like established domains, yet extend them in various ways. Consider, for example, Recipes, which relates to the established domain of Creative Cooking but is not solely focused on drinks and food. Instead, it also incorporates words such as skin, scrub, and mask, indicating the existence of DIY videos about body care. The established domains of Visual and Performing Arts are also partially reflected in the topic Music & Video and Social (i.e., through vlogging). Third, our results reveal additional areas and activities that have been paid lesser attention so far. This includes the realm of beauty through the topics Nail and Hair, but also relates to workshop activities as in Woodworking. The Topics Outdoor Living (e.g., converting a van into a caravan) and Vehicle Repairs form a branch of DIY videos focusing on (restoring) mobility and, perhaps, adventure.

Topic prevalence ranges from 2.48% (Nails) to 6.76% (Woodworking), suggesting that none of the identified topics play an overly dominant role in DIY videos. Notably, the STM approach assigns each video to a mixture of topics (e.g., 70% topic A, 25% topic B, 1.5% topic C, etc.), which implies that some videos are more specific to one topic than others (high maximum γ). Similarly, some topics may involve more general terms that may not actually be specific for this topic (e.g., talk in Business) but still increase the attribution of many videos to this topic and thereby the topic's overall prevalence. To address this issue, we computed an alternative estimation of topical prevalence where we assigned videos to the topic with the highest γ value (e.g., a video contains 70% topic A and is thus assigned to topic A). In general, the estimations of prevalence were largely similar, but some topics (e.g., Fluids) now accounted for a smaller proportion of DIY videos, while others had greater prevalence (e.g., Woodworking; but see Table 1).

sections.paper_id: Ceh et al. - 2025 - D-I-WHAT Identifying creative domains in do-it-yourself videos on YouTube.
paragraphs: Running BERTopic with the previously outlined parameter settings, the number of topics varied substantially (i.e., between 6 and 113 topics) as a function of setting for the minimum required topic size (i.e., how many documents must a topic contain). After considering the resultant topics and the respective tradeoff between coherence and diversity, we found a model with 35 topics most appropriate, which resulted from setting the minimum number of words to seven and minimum topic size to 30 documents. The topics, their prevalence, and representative words are provided in Table 2. A brief look at the identified topics indicates substantial overlap with topics identified by STM and sometimes topics appeared to be broken down into more specific subtopics with BERTopic. For example, the topic Festivities in STM is present in the BERTopic model by means of the topics Christmas, Halloween, and Easter (see Table 2). Similarly, STM's topic Hair is further distinguished in BERTopic by the topics Hair Dye and Hairstyles, and Haircare. Same but different best describes the relationship between the topics Fluids (STM) and Aquatic (BERTopic): While the former appears to be more strongly related to washing and cleaning, the latter more directly refers to organic life such as in a pond or aquarium.

Just as for STM, some of the topics estimated with BERTopic resemble established domains of creative behavior. For example, there is a high prevalence of topics related to Handicrafts (e.g., Small Crafts, Papercrafts, Textiles, Accessories), including a topic that reflects the COVID-19 pandemic: Facemasks. With a higher number of extracted topics, BERTopic provides a more fine-grained perspective on creative content domains as featured in DIY videos on YouTube. Especially a look at small-size topics offers insight into some rarer DIY content topics, such as Candles, arguably a part of Handicrafts. BERTopic also again identified several topics related to Interior and Garden Design as reflected in Makeover, Furniture 1 and 2, Garden, Flowers, and Aquatic. Interestingly, we also discovered a topic related to Sports, a domain that has been occasionally considered as a domain of everyday creative behavior (cf. Carson et al., 2005;Diedrich et al., 2018). Topics such as Skateboards, Small Crafts, Slime, and Experimenting also point toward the role of creativity in playful and leisurely activities.

At the same time, it should be noted that not every topic identified in STM is existent or prevalent to the same extent in BERTopic, and vice versa. Importantly, in BERTopic, a significant proportion of documents was not assigned to a specific topic (29.26%). Such documents were dominated by topic-indiscriminate words such as let, or make (see Topic 99 in Table 2). Interestingly, Woodworking, a topic of high prevalence in STM (6.76%) is of smaller relevance in BERTopic (Woodworking: 0.71%; Furniture 2: 0.71%), with the vast majority of documents being unassigned in BERTopic. Figure 1 graphically represents how documents were classified in STM versus BERTopic. The alluvial plot shows that some topics were largely the same in both topic modeling approaches also with respect to the constituting documents (e.g., STM 16: Garden; BERTopic 1: Garden), whereas other topics were more fragmented in BERTopic (e.g., STM 14: Recipes; BERTopic 3: Cosmetics and 10: Cooking). As a follow-up, we investigated what kinds of documents were unassigned in BERTopic. To this end, we compared the strength of attribution of documents to singular topics in STM (reflected by high maximum γ) between assigned and unassigned documents in BERTopic. Interestingly, the strength of attribution of documents to single topics as identified by STM was lower for unassigned documents (M γ = 0.43; SD = 0.17) than for assigned documents (M γ = 0.50; SD = 0.19; t 5616 = 12.99, p , .001) in BERTopic, suggesting that BERTopic less likely assigned more ambiguous documents to topics.

In a final step, we examined time trends in topical prevalence across both methods of topic modeling. Figure 2 shows a selection of potentially seasonal topics identified with both methods across months in which they were posted on YouTube. Festivity-related topics identified with BERTopic peaked in the expected months (i.e., Easter in April, Halloween in October, and Christmas in December). A similar trend is observed for STM's broader Festivities topic, yet blurred across events that collapsed in this topic. Interestingly, the topic Facemask identified with BERTopic had its highest prevalence in March and April, months that correspond to the COVID-19 outbreak and recurring waves. Again, this time trend was also evident in the STM topic Textiles, which peaked at the same time, although less distinctively. These time dynamics provide clear evidence that the identified topics are timesensitive to expected seasonal variations in related creative behaviors. This pattern supports the content validity of modeled topics, with higher sensitivity for more narrowly defined topics, and points to their potential usefulness for detecting trends in creative behaviors.

sections.paper_id: Ceh et al. - 2025 - D-I-WHAT Identifying creative domains in do-it-yourself videos on YouTube.
paragraphs: In the present study, we examined DIY videos on YouTube as a window to everyday creative activities. Using natural language processing techniques, we analyzed a set of more than 5,000 videos to identify domains of creative engagement in a data-driven way, as well as their associated prevalence, and time-sensitivity. We computed two topic models that represented DIY content at slightly different levels of granularity: STM provided a broader, and BERTopic a more fragmented representation of DIY content, respectively. The identified topics highlight the diversity of creative activities, some of which clearly relate to established creativity domains, while others may extend them. We further analyzed prevalence and seasonal time trends, shedding some light on not only what creative activities people typically engage in but also when.

The STM and BERTopic represent two distinct approaches to extracting the topical composition of text corpora that, depending on the perspective, yielded highly similar, yet also complemental results. The most striking difference was the number of identified topics (STM: 21, BERTopic: 35). STM identified more general content domains which partly reflected established creative domains, as well as more detailed breakdowns of known domains, and entirely new ones. For example, topics such as Decoration, Papercrafts, and Textiles reflect in greater detail, what has been called Arts-And-Crafts (Diedrich et al., 2018), Crafts (CBI; Hocevar, 1979) or Handicrafts (Benedek et al., 2020) before. Moreover, the domain of "Interior/garden design" had been previously identified in open self-reports about creative leisure activities (Benedek et al., 2020), which could be replicated in this study which also identified Garden and Interior Design as a distinct content dimension in DIY videos. This shows that bottom-up approaches indeed identify certain domains of creative everyday behavior that have not been considered by many established self-report inventories of creative behavior. Similarly, Cooking has been considered as creative domain in some inventories (Carson et al., 2005;Diedrich et BERTopic yielded a total of 35 topics, with the smallest topics being composed of less than 1% of the sample. The difference to the number of topics identified with STM may resemble distinguishing domains vs. items. As an example, Accessories as a creative domain may be overly narrow but it may make much sense to include Accessories as an item related to larger domains such as Handicraft. This equally applies to other small topics such as Flowers, or Facemasks. Indeed, BERTopic's granularity helped to identify more specialized forms of creative activity, such as making candles and restoring boats, which highlight the diversity and idiosyncrasy of everyday creativity. Thus, we conclude that in our study, results from STM and BERTopic provide complemental information on slightly different hierarchical levels, which may correspond to different levels of domain-specific differentiation and specialization as proposed by the amusement park theoretical (APT) model of creativity (Baer & Kaufman, 2005;cf. Barbot & Tinio, 2015). Considering both broad and narrow classifications provided by STM and BERTopic together could eventually guide the development of new inventories by suggesting relevant domains and items, respectively.

While the employed bottom-up, data-driven approaches suggested the relevance of some new domains of everyday creative behavior, other typical creative domains, such as literature and performing arts (Silvia et al., 2012), were less prevalent. This raises the question whether these differences could be partly attributed to the specifics of the present approach such as the amenability of DIY, the video modality, or platform biases in YouTube. In other words, while some content might not be well suited for the DIY format (e.g., dancing), other content may not be easily video-recorded (e.g., writing a book), nor deemed popular on the platform and thus not be considered worth committing to. However, the popularity of YouTube (Ceh & Benedek, 2021) and DIY (as evidenced through its economic impact; M. Wolf & McQuitty, 2015) points toward the ecological validity of the present approach. Notably, other studies using bottom-up approaches also found low prevalence for certain typical creative domains such as performing arts and literature (Benedek et al., 2020). Hence, as another possible explanation, some creative domains may be more relevant in professional creativity (e.g., performing arts, literature, science) but less relevant in everyday creativity, whereas everyday creative behavior may involve some domains (e.g., beauty, gardening) that are less prevalent in professional creativity. Going further, some aspects of these broad domains may be captured differently in professional (e.g., writing a scientific article) versus everyday creative activity (e.g., making a bullet journal). As a consequence, we may reconsider whether it is suitable to apply professional creative domains to the Note. We removed links with ,20 documents to increase readability (leading to a reduction to approximately 4,500 documents being displayed in this Figure). Topics are numbered sequentially (STM: 1-21; BERTopic: 1-35 and 99; 99 = unassigned documents). STM = structural topic model. See the online article for the color version of this figure. Analyses of time dynamics for topics across months of the year revealed several plausible trends over time. While this was seen already in broader STM-identified topics (e.g., Festivities), BERTopic-identified topics offered deeper insights into the prevalence of specific behaviors over time. For example, BERTopic topics of Halloween and Christmas showed distinct peaks in October and December, which was also evident by the dominance of Festivities in the final quarter of the year. Similarly, the topic Facemask identified by BERTopic had a major spike in March/April-the time of major COVID waves-which was also reflected in seasonal increases in the general topic Textiles identified with STM. We conclude that while many creative activities appeared stable over time, transient phenomena such as the production of facemasks impacted the extent of creative activities in certain domains, which can be nicely tracked by analyzing dynamics in DIY videos, especially when focusing on more specialized topics as identified with BERTopic. Consequently, the COVID pandemic and more subtle events or trends might have impacted the topical composition of creative behaviors (e.g., Lopez-Persem et al., 2022). These findings highlight the dynamic and partly seasonal nature of everyday creative activities, which has not been given much attention so far. This method could in the future also be applied to study how other major events beyond the COVID pandemic such as political crisis and war affect creative behavior, which could be tracked in real-time or even analyzed post hoc based on available documents such as videos. In this context, however, it will be important to also acknowledge methodological challenges such as that some conflicts actually are accompanied with restrictions of internet access. In sum, the investigation of time-stamped creative content shared online offers a powerful method to study time dynamics and trends in creative behavior.

One important aspect in topic modeling is text preprocessing, a step of data processing that involves many degrees of freedom. Although we adhered to common ways of formatting text in the context of topic modeling and other forms of unsupervised learning (cf. Denny & Spirling, 2018), it must be noted that some aspects could have been handled differently. Similarly, the choice of the topic modeling approach itself has significant implications (e.g., what kind of preprocessing is required) and leads to different outcomes, which is why we chose to report two approaches that differ quite substantially under the hood (see also Egger & Yu, 2022). In topic modeling, as with most natural language processing methods, different parameter settings will lead to different outcomes, which is an important consideration when interpreting results. For example, the estimation of k for the STM involves some ambiguity, as does the choice of settings for BERTopic (e.g., the minimum word requirement for a topic). Specifically, our approach does not allow for any conclusions regarding the true number of content topics nested within DIY videos but rather for some plausible number of topics at different levels of granularity. Moreover, the labeling of the resulting topics was done by the authors based on careful consideration of the underlying words and documents, but others may name certain topics differently. However, the overlapping results from the two employed methods (consider Figures 1 and 2) support the general robustness of our findings.

While the present study sheds new light on the prevalence of everyday creative behaviors, it should be noted that it does not allow to assess how creative the various activities are. DIY activities are generally thought to be creative (Fox, 2013), reflecting everyday creative activities by amateurs (Arthurs et al., 2018) or expert-amateurs (Kuznetsov & Paulos, 2010), yet some videos may convey more Note. Similar trends over time can be observed for some topics as identified with STM (dotted lines) and their counterparts in BERTopic (see also Figure 1 for overlap between topics). DIY = do-it-yourself; STM = structural topic model. See the online article for the color version of this figure.

original ideas or be more creative products than others. Similarly, reproducing previously seen content in new ways can be considered a creative act when it adds new ideas or applications as reflected in the life cycle of a creative product (Literat, 2019). In fact, it is one of the most challenging tasks for creativity research to evaluate the creativity of ideas and products and this equally applies to extracting the creative content among the mountains of online content. A number of likes and views may offer a first rough estimate of a videos popularity which may be associated with its creative quality, but these measures are likely driven by the number of followers and could thus only reflect variation of creative quality within a channel. Future studies could thus aim to assess the creativity of videos (or other types of content) by means of standard procedures such as human ratings and compare it with person-or content-level features (e.g., type of content, professionality; Stein et al., 2022) to arrive at conclusions that may help in automatic classification of highly creative content.

The present study provided a fresh look at everyday creative behavior as exhibited on one of the most popular platforms for posting creative content-YouTube (Ceh & Benedek, 2021). Using a bottom-up, data-driven approach, we investigated DIY videos, a sphere on YouTube that is renowned for its focus on amateur-created content (C. T. Wolf, 2016). Using STM and BERTopic we identified 21 and 35 topics of creative endeavor, respectively, which differed from creative domains usually considered in established creativity inventories. These differences may be partly due to the employed bottom-up approach that is not limited by finding only what it has asked for and thereby offers an update and extension of available accounts of everyday creative behavior. Yet, YouTube offers a specific window on everyday creativity that may be broad but still favor some content over others. The employed methods proved particularly powerful to uncover time dynamics (e.g., facemask production during the COVID pandemic) and seasonality (e.g., festivity-related activities) of everyday creative behaviors. In sum, exploiting freely available creative content online and studying them with timely natural langue processing methods offered an updated account of both the diversity and prevalent domains of everyday creative behavior. It yielded multifaceted insights on topics of creative content, such as the high prevalence of Decoration videos, also showing how some topics clearly follow trends: For example, DIY videos regarding Facemasks were immensely prevalent in months of COVID waves, and DIY videos related to Festivities peak around the time of the occasion. Our study also shows that the creative content showcased on the Internet is different to what is commonly assessed in traditional classifications of creative domains, and also acknowledges difficulties regarding the many layers by which creativity can be conveyed online-be it by means of production, content, or distribution-factors that will ultimately fall into different categories of everyday creative activities. Future studies may specifically focus on the dynamic aspects of creative behavior, which would have exceeded the scope of this article, but would offer a deeper understanding of contextual factors promoting creative behavior.

However, the overlapping results from the two employed methods (consider Figures 1 and 2) support the general robustness of our findings.

While the present study sheds new light on the prevalence of everyday creative behaviors, it should be noted that it does not allow to assess how creative the various activities are. DIY activities are generally thought to be creative (Fox, 2013), reflecting everyday creative activities by amateurs (Arthurs et al., 2018) or expert-amateurs (Kuznetsov & Paulos, 2010), yet some videos may convey more Note. Similar trends over time can be observed for some topics as identified with STM (dotted lines) and their counterparts in BERTopic (see also Figure 1 for overlap between topics). DIY = do-it-yourself; STM = structural topic model. See the online article for the color version of this figure.

original ideas or be more creative products than others. Similarly, reproducing previously seen content in new ways can be considered a creative act when it adds new ideas or applications as reflected in the life cycle of a creative product (Literat, 2019). In fact, it is one of the most challenging tasks for creativity research to evaluate the creativity of ideas and products and this equally applies to extracting the creative content among the mountains of online content. A number of likes and views may offer a first rough estimate of a videos popularity which may be associated with its creative quality, but these measures are likely driven by the number of followers and could thus only reflect variation of creative quality within a channel. Future studies could thus aim to assess the creativity of videos (or other types of content) by means of standard procedures such as human ratings and compare it with person-or content-level features (e.g., type of content, professionality; Stein et al., 2022) to arrive at conclusions that may help in automatic classification of highly creative content.

The present study provided a fresh look at everyday creative behavior as exhibited on one of the most popular platforms for posting creative content-YouTube (Ceh & Benedek, 2021). Using a bottom-up, data-driven approach, we investigated DIY videos, a sphere on YouTube that is renowned for its focus on amateur-created content (C. T. Wolf, 2016). Using STM and BERTopic we identified 21 and 35 topics of creative endeavor, respectively, which differed from creative domains usually considered in established creativity inventories. These differences may be partly due to the employed bottom-up approach that is not limited by finding only what it has asked for and thereby offers an update and extension of available accounts of everyday creative behavior. Yet, YouTube offers a specific window on everyday creativity that may be broad but still favor some content over others. The employed methods proved particularly powerful to uncover time dynamics (e.g., facemask production during the COVID pandemic) and seasonality (e.g., festivity-related activities) of everyday creative behaviors. In sum, exploiting freely available creative content online and studying them with timely natural langue processing methods offered an updated account of both the diversity and prevalent domains of everyday creative behavior. It yielded multifaceted insights on topics of creative content, such as the high prevalence of Decoration videos, also showing how some topics clearly follow trends: For example, DIY videos regarding Facemasks were immensely prevalent in months of COVID waves, and DIY videos related to Festivities peak around the time of the occasion. Our study also shows that the creative content showcased on the Internet is different to what is commonly assessed in traditional classifications of creative domains, and also acknowledges difficulties regarding the many layers by which creativity can be conveyed online-be it by means of production, content, or distribution-factors that will ultimately fall into different categories of everyday creative activities. Future studies may specifically focus on the dynamic aspects of creative behavior, which would have exceeded the scope of this article, but would offer a deeper understanding of contextual factors promoting creative behavior.

In the present study, we examined DIY videos on YouTube as a window to everyday creative activities. Using natural language processing techniques, we analyzed a set of more than 5,000 videos to identify domains of creative engagement in a data-driven way, as well as their associated prevalence, and time-sensitivity. We computed two topic models that represented DIY content at slightly different levels of granularity: STM provided a broader, and BERTopic a more fragmented representation of DIY content, respectively. The identified topics highlight the diversity of creative activities, some of which clearly relate to established creativity domains, while others may extend them. We further analyzed prevalence and seasonal time trends, shedding some light on not only what creative activities people typically engage in but also when.

The STM and BERTopic represent two distinct approaches to extracting the topical composition of text corpora that, depending on the perspective, yielded highly similar, yet also complemental results. The most striking difference was the number of identified topics (STM: 21, BERTopic: 35). STM identified more general content domains which partly reflected established creative domains, as well as more detailed breakdowns of known domains, and entirely new ones. For example, topics such as Decoration, Papercrafts, and Textiles reflect in greater detail, what has been called Arts-And-Crafts (Diedrich et al., 2018), Crafts (CBI; Hocevar, 1979) or Handicrafts (Benedek et al., 2020) before. Moreover, the domain of "Interior/garden design" had been previously identified in open self-reports about creative leisure activities (Benedek et al., 2020), which could be replicated in this study which also identified Garden and Interior Design as a distinct content dimension in DIY videos. This shows that bottom-up approaches indeed identify certain domains of creative everyday behavior that have not been considered by many established self-report inventories of creative behavior. Similarly, Cooking has been considered as creative domain in some inventories (Carson et al., 2005;Diedrich et BERTopic yielded a total of 35 topics, with the smallest topics being composed of less than 1% of the sample. The difference to the number of topics identified with STM may resemble distinguishing domains vs. items. As an example, Accessories as a creative domain may be overly narrow but it may make much sense to include Accessories as an item related to larger domains such as Handicraft. This equally applies to other small topics such as Flowers, or Facemasks. Indeed, BERTopic's granularity helped to identify more specialized forms of creative activity, such as making candles and restoring boats, which highlight the diversity and idiosyncrasy of everyday creativity. Thus, we conclude that in our study, results from STM and BERTopic provide complemental information on slightly different hierarchical levels, which may correspond to different levels of domain-specific differentiation and specialization as proposed by the amusement park theoretical (APT) model of creativity (Baer & Kaufman, 2005;cf. Barbot & Tinio, 2015). Considering both broad and narrow classifications provided by STM and BERTopic together could eventually guide the development of new inventories by suggesting relevant domains and items, respectively.

While the employed bottom-up, data-driven approaches suggested the relevance of some new domains of everyday creative behavior, other typical creative domains, such as literature and performing arts (Silvia et al., 2012), were less prevalent. This raises the question whether these differences could be partly attributed to the specifics of the present approach such as the amenability of DIY, the video modality, or platform biases in YouTube. In other words, while some content might not be well suited for the DIY format (e.g., dancing), other content may not be easily video-recorded (e.g., writing a book), nor deemed popular on the platform and thus not be considered worth committing to. However, the popularity of YouTube (Ceh & Benedek, 2021) and DIY (as evidenced through its economic impact; M. Wolf & McQuitty, 2015) points toward the ecological validity of the present approach. Notably, other studies using bottom-up approaches also found low prevalence for certain typical creative domains such as performing arts and literature (Benedek et al., 2020). Hence, as another possible explanation, some creative domains may be more relevant in professional creativity (e.g., performing arts, literature, science) but less relevant in everyday creativity, whereas everyday creative behavior may involve some domains (e.g., beauty, gardening) that are less prevalent in professional creativity. Going further, some aspects of these broad domains may be captured differently in professional (e.g., writing a scientific article) versus everyday creative activity (e.g., making a bullet journal). Analyses of time dynamics for topics across months of the year revealed several plausible trends over time. While this was seen already in broader STM-identified topics (e.g., Festivities), BERTopic-identified topics offered deeper insights into the prevalence of specific behaviors over time. For example, BERTopic topics of Halloween and Christmas showed distinct peaks in October and December, which was also evident by the dominance of Festivities in the final quarter of the year. Similarly, the topic Facemask identified by BERTopic had a major spike in March/April-the time of major COVID waves-which was also reflected in seasonal increases in the general topic Textiles identified with STM. We conclude that while many creative activities appeared stable over time, transient phenomena such as the production of facemasks impacted the extent of creative activities in certain domains, which can be nicely tracked by analyzing dynamics in DIY videos, especially when focusing on more specialized topics as identified with BERTopic. Consequently, the COVID pandemic and more subtle events or trends might have impacted the topical composition of creative behaviors (e.g., Lopez-Persem et al., 2022). These findings highlight the dynamic and partly seasonal nature of everyday creative activities, which has not been given much attention so far. This method could in the future also be applied to study how other major events beyond the COVID pandemic such as political crisis and war affect creative behavior, which could be tracked in real-time or even analyzed post hoc based on available documents such as videos. In this context, however, it will be important to also acknowledge methodological challenges such as that some conflicts actually are accompanied with restrictions of internet access. In sum, the investigation of time-stamped creative content shared online offers a powerful method to study time dynamics and trends in creative behavior.

Although we adhered to common ways of formatting text in the context of topic modeling and other forms of unsupervised learning (cf. Denny & Spirling, 2018), it must be noted that some aspects could have been handled differently.
